attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old man attends the clinic for review. Three months ago he presented with a one year history of intermittent diarrhoea, abdominal pain and persistent mouth ulcers. A colonoscopy at that time with a terminal ileum biopsy showed ulceration, acute inflammation and non-caseating granulomas. He commenced a tapering course of oral prednisolone and subcutaneous methotrexate following the colonoscopy. In the last month, he has worsening right lower quadrant cramping abdominal pain, persistence of frequent loose unformed stools and mouth ulcers, and had 8 kg of unintentional weight loss, despite attempting to increase his dietary intake. He denies any other medical problems but is now taking loperamide to try and slow down his diarrhoea. He has not been able to attend work for the last month due to the worsening of his symptoms.

His blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg, pulse 65/min, temperature 37.1 °C and respiratory rate 15/min. His BMI is 21. There is mild tenderness in the lower abdomen with normal bowel sounds. There are no anal fissures or fistulas. His stool is negative for occult blood and infections including clostridium dificile. His laboratory investigations show:

  • Haemoglobin: 99 g/L (135-175)

  • Mean corpuscular volume: 82 fL (80-100)

  • White blood cell count: 10.1 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)


Imaging Studies:
A plain abdominal X-ray shows non-specific gas pattern without free air or distended bowel loops.
MR enterography shows thickened mucosa in the distal ileum with adjacent mesenteric stranding. No fistulae, abscesses or free air are present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's clinical presentation is consistent with a diagnosis Crohn's disease. The patient has moderate to severe Crohn disease (anaemia, abdominal tenderness, and weight loss) and has not responded to the initial treatment. He now needs escalated therapy to reduce the risk of complications.

Infliximab is a TNF inhibitor and is considered a highly effective treatment option for inducing and maintaining remission in patients with moderate to severe Crohn's disease. It is a biological therapy which works by neutralising the effects of TNF, a pro-inflammatory cytokine that plays a key role in the pathogenesis of Crohn's disease. Infliximab has been shown to reduce inflammation, promote mucosal healing, and improve symptoms in patients with Crohn's disease. In this case, the patient's symptoms and imaging findings indicate active inflammation in the distal ileum, making infliximab an appropriate choice and the single most effective intervention recommended.

Loperamide is an antidiarrhoeal medication that can help control symptoms of diarrhoea by slowing down the movement of the intestines. While it can be useful for symptomatic relief in some cases of diarrhoea, it does not address the underlying inflammation in Crohn's disease and would not be considered the most effective intervention for this patient with active inflammation and weight loss. The patients dependence on this is a poor prognostic sign.

Azathioprine is an immunosuppressive medication that can be used in the management of Crohn's disease. It works by suppressing the immune system to reduce inflammation. It is less likely to offer control of the patient's symptoms than infliximab; in addition, this medication takes up to six weeks to reach a therapeutic effect and needs to be given alongside steroids.

Surgical resection is not indicated as the patient has no evidence of abscess, stricture, perforation, or fistula. It may be considered in Crohn's disease when medical therapy fails, complications arise (such as strictures, obstructions, or abscesses), or in cases of uncontrolled bleeding.

Ciprofloxacin and metronidazole is more useful in mild disease and will not treat the underlying inflammatory process responsible for the patient's symptoms. These can be used in the treatment of certain complications of Crohn's disease, such as abscesses or fistulas.
learningPoints: Patients with flare-ups of Crohn disease typically present with abdominal pain (right lower quadrant), flatulence/bloating, diarrhoea (can include mucus and blood), fever, weight loss, anaemia.

Infliximab and adalimumab, are recommended as treatment options for adults with severe active Crohn's disease whose disease has not responded to conventional therapy (including immunosuppressive and/or corticosteroid treatments), or who are intolerant of or have contraindications to conventional therapy.
references: Ranasinghe IR, Hsu R. Crohn Disease. [Updated 2023 Feb 20]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-.
Martins R, Carmona C, George B, Epstein J; Guideline Committee. Management of Crohn's disease: summary of updated NICE guidance. BMJ. 2019 Nov 1;367:l5940. doi: 10.1136/bmj.l5940. PMID: 31676715.
